title: Tree mortality in New Mexico tripled in 2025, driven by drought, climate change, insects | Local News | santafenewmexican.com
description: Tree mortality can be a sign that a forest's ecosystem is shifting back into balance — but the ideal density is changing.
bodyText: Forest health program coordinator Victor Lucero with the New Mexico Forestry Division pulls Douglas-fir tussock moth seed pods off a distressed white fir tree while investigating the extent of insect damage to trees in Hyde Memorial State Park. Tree mortality in New Mexico tripled in 2025 as warm temperatures and drought conditions made trees susceptible to insect attack and other stressors, according to a newly released report from the New Mexico Forestry Division. In 2025, the division mapped 209,000 acres of forest killed by attack from bark beetles and other insects, an increase of more than 200% from 2024. Warm temperatures can whip cold-blooded insects into a frenzy, and drought-related stress can make trees particularly vulnerable to attack. “We’re hotter. We’re drier, and that ... exacerbates the whole ecosystem and predisposes the trees to insects and disease,” said Victor Lucero, forest health program manager for the New Mexico Forestry Division. Under normal conditions, native insects like bark beetles might pick off a diseased tree “here or there,” Lucero said. In doing so, they play an important role in the ecosystem by cycling nutrients and decreasing forest density. “The insects do actually bring a benefit to the landscape by reducing the number of trees,” Lucero said. “And those that remain have greater potential to access resources again, primarily water and space.” But 2025 wasn’t a typical year. By the end of the year, more than 70% of the state was in moderate drought, half the state was in severe drought, and New Mexico experienced its second-warmest year on record. According to the report, the number of acres impacted by drought and high heat increased by 66% in 2025. With trees competing for limited water supplies, stressed-out stands are susceptible to larger die-offs. Forest health program coordinator Victor Lucero with the New Mexico Forestry Division chips bark from a dead white fir tree in 2025 in Hyde Memorial State Park to show where burrowing bark beetles have killed the tree . Lucero said it can be shocking to see stands of dead or dying trees. But tree mortality can sometimes be a sign the forest ecosystem is shifting back into balance, he said, bringing too-dense stands back into equilibrium. That ideal density, however, is changing, as long-term drought in the Southwest becomes the new normal and trees compete for limited resources. The impact on the forest is mixed, Lucero said. Initially, a large number of dead trees with their needles still attached can pose an elevated wildfire risk. But that risk diminishes as the needles drop, and even in death, the trees can provide wildlife habitat, cycle nutrients and hold soil in place. Tree mortality can vary based on species and the type of insects in the area. Human-led thinning helps. “If there’s too many trees on the landscape, that’s where reducing the stand through thinning activities can allow the remaining trees to be more resilient and therefore have the potential to withstand or naturally defend against our native insects,” Lucero said. Victor Lucero, forest health program coordinator with the New Mexico Forestry Division, investigates the extent of insect damage to trees in Hyde Memorial State Forest in 2025. But unlike human thinning projects, insects are indiscriminate in their attacks. While Lucero said there are some solutions, including insect repellants, there are few options to combat the root cause: drought and warming temperatures. Homeowners can water their trees a bit more, he said, but that’s not feasible on a forest-wide scale. “There’s not a whole lot that one can do,” Lucero said. He expects the upcoming year will be similar, unless there’s a boost of moisture that allows trees to become more resilient to insect attack. “We continue to have dry conditions as we speak,” Lucero said. That means areas that have already experienced insect activity could continue to be impacted. “The very best that we can hope for is a stellar monsoon season,” Lucero said.
